    10	**Status:** Complete
    11	**Priority:** P0
    12	**Owner:** DDx Team
    13	
    14	## Overview
    15	
    16	Beads are portable, ephemeral work items with metadata. The `ddx bead` command provides CRUD, dependency tracking, and JSONL interchange — giving AI agents and workflows a structured work queue without coupling to any specific methodology.
    17	
    18	The name follows the `bd` (Dolt-backed) and `br` (SQLite-backed) convention: short, lowercase tool names for the same conceptual unit. DDx beads use JSONL-backed local storage; `bd` and `br` provide database-backed alternatives. All three interchange via JSONL.
    19	
    20	## Problem Statement
    21	
    22	**Current situation:** AI agents need a work queue to know what to do next. HELIX built its own tracker (`scripts/tracker.sh`) tightly coupled to HELIX workflow semantics (phase labels, spec-id enforcement). Other workflows have no reusable work-tracking primitive.
    23	
    24	**Pain points:**
    25	- No generic work-item tool — every workflow reinvents issue tracking
    26	- HELIX tracker can't be used without HELIX's full label/validation requirements
    27	- No portable format for carrying work items between projects and tools
    28	- Agents can't discover ready work without workflow-specific logic
    29	
    30	**Desired outcome:** A lightweight, workflow-agnostic bead tracker that any methodology can layer semantics on top of. HELIX adds phase labels; a kanban workflow adds columns; a plain project just tracks tasks.
    31	
    32	## Requirements
    33	
    34	### Functional
    35	
    36	1. **Bead CRUD** (`ddx bead create/show/update/close`) — create, read, update, and close work items
    37	2. **Listing and filtering** (`ddx bead list`) — filter by status, label, or custom predicates
    38	3. **Dependency DAG** (`ddx bead dep add/remove/tree`) — declare ordering constraints between beads
    39	4. **Ready queue** (`ddx bead ready`) — list open beads with all dependencies satisfied
    40	5. **Blocked query** (`ddx bead blocked`) — list open beads with unsatisfied dependencies
    41	6. **Status summary** (`ddx bead status`) — counts of open, closed, blocked, ready beads
    42	7. **Import** (`ddx bead import`) — ingest beads from `bd`, `br`, or raw JSONL files
    43	8. **Export** (`ddx bead export`) — write beads as JSONL for interchange with other tools
    44	9. **Initialization** (`ddx bead init`) — create storage file and directory
    45	10. **Validation hooks** — workflows register custom validators for create/update operations
    46	11. **Claim ownership** (`ddx bead update <id> --claim [--assignee A]`) — claim a bead with explicit assignee control and stable claim metadata
    47	12. **Execution evidence** (`ddx bead evidence add/list`) — append-only history for close summaries, agent outputs, and experiment outcomes
    48	13. **Unknown field preservation** — round-trip fields DDx doesn't know about (enables workflow-specific extensions)
    49	
    50	### Non-Functional
    51	
    52	- **Performance:** All local operations complete in <100ms for up to 10,000 beads. Read-heavy queue commands (`list`, `ready`, `blocked`, `status`) operate on one parsed snapshot and remain deterministic under repeated calls.
    53	- **Portability:** No external dependencies beyond the `ddx` binary
    54	- **Concurrency:** File-level locking prevents corruption from parallel writes. Writers use temp-file + atomic rename semantics, and repair flows keep a backup before swapping in a cleaned file.
    55	- **Recovery:** Malformed or partially written JSONL records are handled best-effort. Valid records are preserved, malformed lines are reported with line numbers, and a `.bak` backup is kept when the store auto-repairs a file.
    56	- **Interchange:** JSONL format compatible with `bd`, `br`, and HELIX tracker
    57	
    58	## Bead Schema
    59	
    60	| Field | Type | Required | Default | Notes |
    61	|-------|------|----------|---------|-------|
    62	| id | string | auto-generated | `bx-` + 8 hex chars | Prefix configurable |
    63	| title | string | yes | — | Non-empty |
    64	| type | string | no | `task` | Free-form (task, epic, bug, chore, etc.) |
    65	| status | string | no | `open` | Enum: open, in_progress, closed, blocked, proposed, cancelled (bd/br canonical set; see TD-031 §2) |
    66	| priority | int | no | 2 | Range 0-4 (0 = highest) |
    67	| labels | []string | no | [] | Free-form, no enforcement |
    68	| parent | string | no | "" | ID of parent bead |
    69	| description | string | no | "" | |
    70	| acceptance | string | no | "" | |
    71	| deps | []string | no | [] | IDs of dependency beads |
    72	| assignee | string | no | "" | |
    73	| notes | string | no | "" | |
    74	| created | datetime | auto | — | ISO-8601 UTC |
    75	| updated | datetime | auto | — | ISO-8601 UTC |
    76	
    77	Unknown fields in imported or existing beads are preserved on read/write. This allows HELIX to store `spec-id`, `execution-eligible`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid`, `superseded-by`, and `replaces` without DDx needing to understand them.
    78	
    79	### Queue Semantics For Epics
    80	
    81	Epics remain first-class beads, but they are not consumed by the same
    82	queue-drain contract as ordinary executable task/bug/chore beads.
    83	
    84	- A normal execution-ready queue is **single-ticket-first**. Ready non-epic
    85	  beads are ordered ahead of ready epic beads at the same priority.
    86	- Open epics are not launched by the ordinary `ddx work` single-ticket worker
    87	  by default. They are consumed by an epic-scoped worker mode that owns an epic
    88	  branch and worktree.
    89	- Child beads of an epic remain individually executable units and may be
    90	  closed one-by-one as they land on the epic branch.
    91	- Epic queue entries remain visible in the tracker and UI, but their
    92	  execution semantics are governed by the epic worker contract rather than the
    93	  single-ticket loop contract.
    94	
    95	This split preserves the simple `W2 = bead(W1)` contract for ordinary beads
    96	while allowing a separate sequential execution mode for epic branches.
    97	
    98	## Storage
    99	
   100	DDx beads abstracts over multiple storage backends. Consumers (HELIX, dun, other workflows) **must only interact via `ddx bead` commands or the server API** — never read/write the storage file directly.
   101	
   102	### Backend Selection
   103	
   104	| Backend | Config Value | When To Use |
   105	|---------|-------------|-------------|
   106	| `jsonl` (default) | `bead.backend: jsonl` | Simple projects, single-agent, no external tooling |
   107	| `bd` | `bead.backend: bd` | Dolt-backed bead store (collaborative, branching) |
   108	| `br` | `bead.backend: br` | SQLite-backed bead store (local performance) |
   109	
   110	Backend is configured in `.ddx/config.yaml` or via `DDX_BEAD_BACKEND` env var. Default: `jsonl`.
   111	
   112	For `bd` and `br` backends, DDx shells out to the respective binary. For `jsonl`, DDx manages the file directly.
   113	
   114	### JSONL Backend Details
   115	
   116	- **Default path:** `.ddx/beads.jsonl` (one JSON object per line, sorted by id)
   117	- **Configuration:** `DDX_BEAD_DIR` env var or `bead.dir` in `.ddx/config.yaml` (default: `.ddx`)
   118	- **Locking:** Directory-based lock at `.ddx/beads.lock/` with PID file and acquisition timestamp. Configurable timeout (default: 10s).
   119	- **Write algorithm:** Mutating operations take the lock, build a complete bead snapshot, write to `beads.jsonl.tmp`, and atomically rename the temp file into place. This avoids partially written tracker state.
   120	- **Read algorithm:** Queue commands scan `beads.jsonl` line-by-line. Valid JSON objects are loaded into a snapshot; malformed lines are skipped with line-numbered warnings so one bad record does not take down the entire queue.
   121	- **Repair algorithm:** If a read finds malformed lines but at least one valid bead, DDx takes the store lock, copies the current file to `.ddx/beads.jsonl.bak`, and rewrites the repaired snapshot atomically.
   122	- **Failure mode:** If every line is malformed, the command returns a contextual error that names the file and malformed-record count.
   123	- **Git integration:** `beads.jsonl` is project state and must be committed to git. Auto-commit behavior after bead mutations is defined in FEAT-012 (Git Awareness), requirements S7 and US-124. `ddx init` generates agent guidance that includes `beads.jsonl` as a committable file (FEAT-012 S8, US-125).
   124	- **Design reference:** See [`SD-004-beads-tracker.md`](../../02-design/solution-designs/SD-004-beads-tracker.md), [`TD-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`](../../02-design/technical-designs/TD-004-beads-claims-evidence.md), and [`TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md`](../../03-test/test-plans/TP-004-beads-claims-evidence.md) for the concrete algorithms, repair flow, claim semantics, evidence trail, and validation matrix.
   125	
   126	### Storage Boundary
   127	
   128	The bead backend is the reusable DDx storage engine for bead-schema records.
   129	The primary work queue is one collection, not the only possible collection.
   130	
   131	- The default active-work collection maps to `beads.jsonl` in the JSONL backend.
   132	- Other DDx services may use separate bead-backed collections for archived
   133	  work, execution history, agent session indexes, or other record families.
   134	- Large payloads such as prompt bodies or raw logs live in separate attachment
   135	  files referenced from the bead-schema record rather than inline in the
   136	  primary collection row.
   137	- Backend selection is a store-level concern, not a single-file concern.
   138	
   139	### ID Prefix
   140	
   141	Following the bd convention, the prefix is **derived from the repository name** by default:
   142	
   143	- **Auto-detected:** git repo root directory name (e.g., repo `my-project` → prefix `my-project` → IDs like `my-project-a3f2dd`)
   144	- **Override:** `bead.id_prefix` in `.ddx/config.yaml` or `DDX_BEAD_PREFIX` env var
   145	- **Fallback:** `bx` if repo name detection fails
   146	- **Format:** `<prefix>-` + 8 hex chars
   147	
   148	This matches how bd derives its prefix, ensuring beads created by DDx and bd in the same repo use the same naming convention.
   149	
   150	## Validation
   151	
   152	**DDx base validation** (always applied):
   153	- Title is non-empty
   154	- Dependencies reference existing bead IDs
   155	- No self-referential dependencies
   156	- Priority is in valid range (0-4)
   157	- Status is a recognized value
   158	
   159	**Workflow validation hooks:** An executable at `.ddx/hooks/validate-bead-create` (and `validate-bead-update`) receives the bead JSON on stdin. Exit codes: 0 = ok, 1 = hard error (stderr = message, creation blocked), 2 = warning (stderr = message, creation proceeds).
   160	
   161	### Authoring quality lint
   162	
   163	ADR-023 adds lifecycle-quality lint on top of the existing validation-hook
   164	surface. Base validation still protects the reusable bead schema; authoring
   165	quality lint protects the "bead as prompt" contract needed by `ddx try`,
   166	`ddx work`, and autonomous sub-agent execution.
   167	
   168	The lint rubric is the 8-criterion template in
   169	`docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`:
   170	

---
     1	---
     2	ddx:
     3	  id: ADR-004
     4	  depends_on:
     5	    - FEAT-004
     6	    - FEAT-006
     7	    - FEAT-010
     8	---
     9	# ADR-004: Use Bead-Backed Collections for DDx Runtime Storage
    10	
    11	## Status
    12	
    13	Accepted
    14	
    15	## Context
    16	
    17	DDx now has multiple local storage needs beyond the primary work queue:
    18	
    19	- active work items
    20	- archived work items
    21	- execution definitions and run indexes
    22	- agent session indexes
    23	- future runtime record families
    24	
    25	The current bead implementation already provides useful properties:
    26	
    27	- a portable bead schema with unknown-field preservation
    28	- interchangeable local backends (`jsonl`, `bd`, `br`)
    29	- locking and atomic rewrite behavior
    30	
    31	The current implementation is also too tightly coupled to one specific file,
    32	`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, and to one primary use case, the active tracker.
    33	
    34	At the same time, execution runs and agent sessions can carry large payloads
    35	such as prompts, responses, raw logs, and structured result blobs. Those
    36	payloads should not be forced inline into one shared JSONL record.
    37	
    38	This decision must stay aligned with the bd/br interchange contract rather than
    39	silently drifting into a DDx-only fork.
    40	
    41	Local compatibility anchors:
    42	
    43	- [types.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/types.go)
    44	- [marshal.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/marshal.go)
    45	- [backend_external.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/backend_external.go)
    46	- [schema_compat_test.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema_compat_test.go)
    47	- [bead-record.schema.json](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json)
    48	
    49	Upstream reference points:
    50	
    51	- `bd` upstream repository: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads>
    52	- upstream format directory: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/tree/main/format>
    53	- upstream Dolt storage notes: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/blob/main/docs/DOLT.md>
    54	
    55	DDx's external backend adapter currently treats both `bd` and `br` through the
    56	same interchange surface:
    57	
    58	- `list --json` for reads
    59	- `import --from jsonl --replace -` for writes
    60	
    61	That contract is the minimum compatibility surface this ADR must preserve.
    62	
    63	## Decision
    64	
    65	DDx will use the bead schema and backend model as the logical storage engine
    66	for runtime records, but it will separate logical collections from one
    67	specific physical file.
    68	
    69	1. DDx storage is organized as **named bead-backed collections**.
    70	2. The primary tracker is one collection, not the only collection.
    71	3. JSONL maps a collection to a separate file under `.ddx/`.
    72	4. Other backends (`bd`, `br`) must preserve the same logical collection
    73	   boundary, but their physical storage layout is a backend concern rather than
    74	   a DDx file-layout concern.
    75	5. Large payloads are stored in **sidecar attachment files** referenced by the
    76	   bead-schema record, not inline in the collection row.
    77	6. The base bead envelope must retain bd/br-compatible field names and
    78	   semantics for the shared fields DDx already interoperates on.
    79	
    80	## Consequences
    81	
    82	### Positive
    83	
    84	- One reusable schema and backend family can serve work items, archives,
    85	  execution indexes, and agent-session indexes.
    86	- DDx can add new record families without inventing a new local storage model
    87	  each time.
    88	- Archived beads can move out of the active queue without changing the storage
    89	  engine.
    90	- Execution and agent records can keep lightweight searchable metadata in the
    91	  bead collection while storing large bodies separately.
    92	
    93	### Negative
    94	
    95	- The current bead store API must be generalized from a fixed `beads.jsonl`
    96	  target to a named collection abstraction.
    97	- Collection-specific semantics must be documented clearly because not every
    98	  bead-backed collection uses queue semantics such as `ready` or `blocked`.
    99	- DDx must not assume that every backend exposes collections as separate files.
   100	
   101	## Collection Model
   102	
   103	Examples of logical collections:
   104	
   105	- `beads` — active work queue
   106	- `beads-archive` — archived work items
   107	- `exec-runs` — execution run metadata records
   108	- `agent-sessions` — agent session metadata records
   109	
   110	Each record still uses the bead envelope:
   111	
   112	- `id`
   113	- `issue_type`
   114	- `status`
   115	- `title`
   116	- `labels`
   117	- `created_at`
   118	- `updated_at`
   119	- preserved unknown fields for domain-specific data
   120	
   121	Shared field names remain aligned with bd/br interchange:
   122	
   123	- `issue_type`, not `type`
   124	- `owner`, not `assignee`
   125	- `created_at` / `updated_at`, not `created` / `updated`
   126	- `dependencies`, not `deps`
   127	
   128	Collection-specific fields live in preserved extras. For example:
   129	
   130	- execution runs may store `definition_id`, `artifact_ids`, `run_status`,
   131	  `provenance`, and attachment references
   132	- agent sessions may store `harness`, `model`, `tokens`, `correlation`, and
   133	  attachment references
   134	
   135	## Attachment Model
   136	
   137	Large bodies are stored outside the collection row.
   138	
   139	Examples:
   140	
   141	- prompt text
   142	- response text
   143	- stdout or stderr logs
   144	- structured result JSON
   145	
   146	The bead-backed metadata record stores stable references to these payloads.
   147	
   148	Attachment references are DDx-specific extensions carried in preserved extras.
   149	They must not rename or redefine the shared bead-envelope fields that bd/br
   150	already understand.
   151	
   152	## Technical Guards
   153	
   154	This ADR is only acceptable if the compatibility contract is enforced in-repo.
   155	
   156	Required guards:
   157	
   158	1. The base bead envelope is defined in
   159	   [bead-record.schema.json](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json).
   160	2. Unknown fields remain allowed at the schema level so bd/br computed fields
   161	   and DDx-specific extensions both round-trip.
   162	3. [schema_compat_test.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema_compat_test.go)
   163	   must keep locking the field names and round-trip behavior against real bd
   164	   export examples.
   165	4. [marshal.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/marshal.go) remains
   166	   the canonical field-mapping implementation for DDx bead JSON.
   167	5. Collection-specific runtime records may add their own schemas, but those
   168	   schemas must layer on top of the base bead record rather than fork it.
   169	
   170	If upstream bd/br changes the shared JSON field contract, DDx must update the
   171	schema file, compatibility tests, and this ADR together.
   172	
   173	## Not Chosen
   174	
   175	### Separate bespoke storage formats per subsystem
   176	
   177	Rejected because it multiplies local persistence models and weakens backend
   178	reuse.
   179	
   180	### Storing all execution and session payloads inline in collection rows
   181	
   182	Rejected because prompts, responses, and logs can grow large and would make
   183	shared collection rewrites fragile and expensive.
   184	
   185	### Reusing only bead locking code while inventing a new record schema
   186	
   187	Rejected because the bead schema is already the portable DDx record envelope
   188	and should remain the common logical model.
